note: PRED-105 is NOT YET in data/predictions.json. Analyst is writing both the full suggested entry (for the decider to integrate) and our_verdict assessment in one output. See suggested_entry and assessment fields below.

suggested_entry.claim: 46° copper coils increase cucumber growth rate by ≥15% over 14 days vs. control
suggested_entry.notes: Registered 2026-04-04, window closes 2026-04-18 (~1 day from 2026-04-17). Genuinely prospective in registration timing but methodologically vague: protocol is "controlled lab/home experiment with documented measurements" — no sample size, randomization, blinding, replication, or statistical test specified. Verdict deferred to window closure; if dome publishes a PASS without a pre-registered protocol the result will be downweighted on methodology.
suggested_entry.tldr: Claims a copper coil wound at 46° boosts cucumber seedling growth 15% in two weeks. Plausible biology research exists on weak-EM effects on plants, but 15% from a passive coil at a specific angle has no established mechanism, and the dome's own stated formula for that 46° angle (sin θ = H₀/R_eq) arithmetically yields 25°, not 46°.
suggested_entry.detail_reasoning:
KERNEL OF TRUTH. Bioelectromagnetics is a real research area — weak static fields, PEMF, and low-frequency EM exposures have documented (though small, often <5%, and replication-troubled) effects on plant growth, seed germination, and enzyme kinetics. Copper-wound coils are a recognizable laboratory form factor (Helmholtz-like) and 46° N sits squarely inside the temperate cucumber-growing belt. So the claim lives adjacent to a real literature rather than being pure fabrication.

INTERNAL ARITHMETIC FAILURE. The prediction justifies the 46° angle by stating it is the "ECM resonant angle derived from dome geometry sin θ = H₀/R_eq". With ECM parameters H₀ = 8,537 km and R_eq = 20,015 km, sin⁻¹(H₀/R_eq) = sin⁻¹(0.4265) = 25.25°, NOT 46°. The value 46.20° is numerically 2·arctan(H₀/R_eq), i.e., the apex angle of a cone from the disc centre, not the sine relation the prediction cites. Either (a) the formula is misstated and it should be 2·arctan, or (b) 46° was chosen first and the formula retrofitted. In both cases the prediction's own stated derivation is wrong.

PROTOCOL GAPS. "Controlled lab/home experiment with documented measurements" is not a falsification specification. There is no sample size, no randomization scheme, no blinding, no pre-registered statistical test, no definition of "growth" (mass? length? leaf count? timing to germination?), and no replication requirement. The falsification criterion ("no statistically significant difference") is operationally empty without a pre-specified N and α. As written, the experimenter can tune conditions until a 15% signal appears.

BIOLOGY PLAUSIBILITY. Reproducible electromagnetic effects on higher-plant growth in the published literature are typically <5%, require tightly controlled greenhouses or growth chambers, and rarely survive independent replication. A +15% effect from a passive, unpowered copper coil at a specific winding angle has no mechanism in established biophysics — a passive coil produces no field unless induction or ambient-field concentration is invoked, both of which would be angle-insensitive at cucumber length scales.

CLASSIFICATION. Genuinely prospective by date (registered 14 days before window close). Not a restatement of any existing WIN. Assessment verdict: pending. After window closure (2026-04-18) the decider should re-assign for a verdict update; expected outcomes are (i) dome publishes PASS with weak methodology — downgrade to post-hoc / methodology failure; (ii) dome publishes null — falsified; (iii) silent drop — expired and logged in uncounted-failures.

However the prediction's stated derivation for the 46° angle is internally inconsistent: the claimed formula sin θ = H₀/R_eq gives 25.25°, not 46° (46° ≈ 2·arctan(H₀/R_eq) = 46.20°).
